title: Trauma-Sensitive Yoga and the Anger That Arises
dek: *When you finally feel safe enough to soften, the anger you've been holding in your hips, jaw, and shoulders may flood forward. Trauma-sensitive yoga doesn't cause this rage—it creates the conditions for your body to finally let it go. Here's how to work with the heat when it arrives, and why that arrival is worth honoring.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Anger that surfaces during trauma-sensitive yoga is not a setback but a sign your nervous system trusts the practice enough to release what it has held. The body stores rage in fascia, breath patterns, and postural habits until safe movement creates an opening. Learning to stay present with that heat without collapsing or exploding is the work itself.

The anger isn't new. It's been living in your body the whole time, coiled in the tension along your iliacus, locked into the set of your jaw, woven through the shallow sips of breath you've been taking for months or years. Trauma-sensitive yoga doesn't create anger - it creates enough safety that your nervous system finally feels permitted to acknowledge what's already there. And when that permission arrives, the heat can be startling. You might be halfway through a supported child's pose, forehead resting on a bolster, when a wave of inexplicable fury rises. Or you're in a gentle twist and suddenly you want to shove the mat away, leave the room, never come back. In our experience, this is one of the most common - and most misunderstood - responses in trauma-informed movement work. People think they're doing something wrong. They're not. They're doing something deeply, almost radically right. ## Why Anger Lives in the Body First Your body experienced the original event before your mind had language for it. Whether the trauma was a single incident or a slow accumulation, your nervous system encoded the threat in muscle memory, breath restriction, and postural collapse. Anger is part of that encoding. It's the biological mobilization response that got interrupted, the "fight" that never completed, the boundary that wanted to assert itself but couldn't. Karla McLaren, in her work on emotional flow, describes anger as a boundary-setting emotion that contains vital information about what we value and where our limits have been crossed. When trauma occurs, that boundary information doesn't disappear - it goes underground. Your psoas holds it. Your diaphragm grips around it. The small muscles between your ribs learn to brace against it. Trauma-sensitive yoga, with its emphasis on interoception and choice, invites you back into your body on your own terms. You're not being pushed into shapes. You're being offered options. And paradoxically, that offering of choice is what allows the defensive anger to surface. When you're finally not in danger, the body can afford to feel what it couldn't afford to feel before. Over the years I've seen students burst into tears during savasana, not from sadness but from a kind of molten rage they didn't know they were carrying. The relaxation wasn't the problem. The relaxation was the doorway. ## The Difference Between Anger and Rage in Somatic Practice It helps to distinguish between anger as information and rage as an overwhelm state. Anger, in its clearest form, is specific. It has an object. It knows what it's responding to. Rage is more diffuse, more total-body, more likely to flood your system and leave you feeling out of control. Both can arise in trauma-sensitive practice, but they call for different responses. When anger arises and you can still track your breath, still notice the sensation in your chest or hands, still feel your feet on the ground - that's workable anger. You're in your window of tolerance. You can stay with it, breathe into it, let it move. When rage arrives and you lose that tracking, when your vision narrows or your thoughts spiral into a loop of catastrophe, you've likely moved into hyperarousal. That's the moment to pause, to resource, to bring your attention back to something stable - the weight of your body on the mat, the texture of the blanket under your palm, the sound in the room. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger is like a crying baby. You don't ignore it, and you don't let it run the house. You hold it with tenderness, the way you'd hold an infant, recognizing that beneath the noise is a need. In the context of trauma-sensitive yoga, that means you meet the anger with curiosity instead of judgment. What is this heat trying to protect? What boundary is it trying to restore? ## What Happens in the Tissue When Anger Releases The fascia - that connective web that wraps every muscle, organ, and nerve in your body - is both a storage site and a communication network. When you experience a threat and can't complete the protective response, the fascia tightens to brace for impact. That tightening can persist for years, creating what some bodyworkers call "tissue memory." When you move slowly and with agency, when you breathe into a hip opener or allow your shoulders to drop away from your ears, the fascia begins to reorganize. As it does, it can release not just tension but the emotional content that was stored alongside that tension. You might feel a wash of heat up your spine. Your hands might clench. Your breath might catch and then deepen suddenly, as if something opened. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load shows that suppressed anger increases blood pressure and heart rate variability in ways that chronically tax the system. When anger is allowed to surface and move - not acted out in harm, but acknowledged and felt - the cardiovascular system begins to recalibrate. Your heart rate might spike briefly as the emotion peaks, then settle into a steadier rhythm as the wave passes. This is not re-traumatization. Re-traumatization happens when you're pushed past your capacity to integrate. Supported release happens when you have enough ground beneath you to let something go without falling apart. Trauma-sensitive yoga is designed to offer that ground - through props, through pacing, through the teacher's consistent invitation to choose what feels right in your own body. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Working with Anger on the Mat Find a comfortable seat or lie on your back with your knees bent, feet flat. Place one hand on your belly and one hand on your chest. Close your eyes if that feels okay, or soften your gaze toward the floor. Take three slow breaths, noticing where you feel the breath most easily - under your top hand, your bottom hand, or somewhere else entirely. No need to change anything yet. Just notice. Now bring to mind a moment when you felt a flicker of anger recently. Not the biggest rage of your life - just a small, manageable irritation. Notice where that memory lands in your body. Does your jaw tighten? Does your breath get shallow? Do your fists want to clench? Stay with that sensation for five breaths. If it gets too big, open your eyes and press your feet into the floor. If it feels workable, breathe into the place where the anger lives. Imagine the breath moving around it, not to make it go away but to give it space. On your next exhale, make a sound - any sound. A sigh, a hum, a low growl if that's what wants to come. Let the vibration carry some of the heat out of your body. Repeat twice more. Finish by placing both hands over your heart and taking three more breaths, thanking your body for holding what it needed to hold, and for beginning to let it go. ## When the Teacher Needs to Know Not every burst of anger in a yoga class needs to be announced, but there are moments when it's worth signaling to your teacher that something big is moving. If you feel like you might need to leave the room, a simple hand signal - a thumbs-up or thumbs-down check-in - can let the teacher know you're self-regulating. If you're in a one-on-one or small-group setting, a quiet "I'm feeling some heat, I might need to pause" gives the teacher context. A trauma-sensitive teacher won't ask you to explain, perform, or justify what you're feeling. They'll offer options: Do you want to stay in the shape you're in, shift to something more grounding, or take a break entirely? The goal is never to push through. The goal is to build your capacity to be with what arises, at a pace your nervous system can handle. If you're working with anger outside of class - if it's showing up in your daily life in ways that feel unmanageable - it may be worth exploring [the assessments hub](/assessments) to get a clearer picture of your nervous system patterns. Sometimes the anger that surfaces in yoga is the tip of a much larger iceberg, and having a map of your own reactivity can help you navigate with more skill. ## Herbs and Supports for the Aftermath After a practice where anger has moved through, your system may need some tending. You might feel wrung out, shaky, or surprisingly calm. All of those are normal. This is where gentle nervous system support can help you integrate what just happened. [Banyan Botanicals Gotu Kola Powder (3.5 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008X9JM68?tag=spankyspinola-20) is one of the herbs we return to again and again for post-release grounding. In Ayurvedic tradition, gotu kola is considered a medhya rasayana - a rejuvenative for the mind and nervous system. It doesn't sedate, but it does help knit things back together after they've come undone. A half teaspoon stirred into warm milk or water before bed can support the kind of deep rest your body needs after big emotional work. If you're drawn to flower essences, [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Oak - Restore Endurance - Natural Homeopathic Flo](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005P0UPGY?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be helpful in the days following an intense release. Oak essence is for those who've been holding everything together through sheer will, and it offers a kind of permission to soften without collapsing. Four drops under the tongue or in water, as needed. You can explore more options in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where we keep profiles of plants that work specifically with the aftermath of anger and the restoration of resilience. ## The Anger That Protects and the Anger That Teaches Thomas Moore, in his writing on the soul's emotional life, suggests that anger is often a guardian of something we love. It arises when a core value has been violated, when something precious has been disregarded. In trauma, that "something precious" is often your own body, your own boundary, your own right to take up space and say no. When anger surfaces in trauma-sensitive yoga, it's worth asking: What is this anger protecting? What part of me is it trying to defend? Sometimes the answer is immediate. Sometimes it takes weeks of practice to clarify. Either way, the anger is not the enemy. It's the alarm system that's finally allowed to sound. Across the articles we've published on this site, one theme returns again and again: the body doesn't lie, but it doesn't always speak in words. Anger is one of its languages. Learning to listen without flinching, without shame, without the need to fix it immediately - that's the heart of trauma-sensitive practice. If you want to go deeper into the theory and application of body-based trauma work, [Sensorimotor Psychotherapy - Pat Ogden](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0393708810?tag=spankyspinola-20) is an essential text. Ogden's framework for working with sensation, movement, and meaning is foundational to much of what we do in trauma-sensitive spaces, and it offers a roadmap for understanding why anger shows up in the body first. ## Building a Container Strong Enough to Hold the Heat You can't do this work without support. That support might be a skilled teacher, a therapist trained in somatic modalities, a trusted friend who understands trauma, or a practice community that doesn't pathologize big feelings. It might also be the structure you build in your own life - regular sleep, nourishing food, time in nature, practices that help you track your window of tolerance. [The fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) offers practices specifically designed for working with the heat of anger, rage, and frustration in ways that don't harm yourself or others. These aren't about suppression. They're about building the capacity to feel the full intensity of your emotional life without getting consumed by it. Some days, that capacity will be larger. Other days, it will be smaller. Trauma recovery is not linear, and neither is your relationship with anger. There will be practices where nothing surfaces, and practices where you feel like you could burn the whole room down. Both are information. Both are part of the process. The goal is not to eliminate anger. The goal is to restore your agency in relationship to it - to know that you can feel it, breathe with it, let it move, and come back to center. That's not bypassing. That's integration. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is it normal to feel angrier after starting trauma-sensitive yoga than I did before? Yes, and it's often a sign that your nervous system is beginning to thaw. When you've been in shutdown or freeze for a long time, the first stage of coming back online can involve a surge of activation - including anger. Your body is waking up, and with that waking comes all the feelings that were put on hold. This phase can be uncomfortable, but it's typically temporary. If the anger feels unmanageable or unsafe, work with a trauma-informed therapist alongside your movement practice. ### Should I stop practicing if anger keeps coming up? Not necessarily. The question is whether you have enough support to stay with what's arising. If you're practicing alone and the anger is overwhelming, it may be worth shifting to a class environment where a trained teacher can help you resource. If you're already in a supported setting and the anger is workable - meaning you can track it, breathe with it, and come back to ground - then continuing is often the best way to build capacity. Trust your own discernment, and don't hesitate to pause if you need to. ### How do I know if what I'm feeling is old anger from trauma or just frustration with the practice itself? Sometimes you won't know right away, and that's okay. Old anger often has a quality of intensity that's disproportionate to the present moment - like a ten-out-of-ten reaction to a two-out-of-ten trigger. It might also come with body memories, intrusive thoughts, or a sense of being flooded. Frustration with the practice tends to be more specific and present-tense: "This teacher's cues are confusing," or "I wish I could do this pose differently." Both are valid, and both deserve attention. If you're unsure, bring curiosity to the sensation itself rather than trying to label it right away. ## What Comes After the Heat There's a moment, sometimes days after a big release, when you notice that your shoulders sit differently. Or you take a full breath without thinking about it. Or you say no to something and it doesn't feel like the end of the world. These are the quiet aftershocks of anger that has finally been allowed to move. Trauma-sensitive yoga doesn't promise that you'll never feel angry again. It offers something subtler and more sustainable: the possibility that you can feel anger without being overtaken by it, that you can honor its message without letting it run your life, that you can hold heat in your body without burning down. The practice continues. The anger will come and go. And each time it does, you'll have a little more skill, a little more ground, a little more capacity to meet it as a teacher rather than an enemy. That's not transcendence. That's embodiment. And it's worth every difficult breath along the way. For more explorations of the emotional life in the body, visit [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where we track the slower, stranger unfoldings that don't always fit into a single practice or a single essay. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on trauma-sensitive yoga and the anger that arises, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
